content.problemStatement:
The tortoise and the hare decide to run a race, yet again.

This time around however, the hare has gotten a bit smarter!

The racetrack is a straight line, LLL meters long.

The tortoise moves at V1V_1V1​ meters per second, while the hare moves at V2V_2V2​ meters per second. It is known that V1<V2V_1 \lt V_2V1​<V2​, that is, the hare is strictly faster.

The hare still wants to be a bit lazy, so it decides to give the tortoise a headstart — the hare will wait for an **integer** number of seconds before starting to run.

Unfortunately, the animals' measuring devices aren't up to par — they can only measure in integer seconds, and will round up — so for example:


If the hare takes 1.571.571.57 seconds to finish, the reported time will be 222 seconds.
If the hare takes 333 seconds to finish, the reported time will be 333 seconds.
If the hare takes 3.013.013.01 seconds to finish, the reported time will be 444 seconds.
What's the **longest** time the hare can wait, while still being able to win the race?

Note that to win the race, the hare's reported time must be strictly less than the tortoise's.

If the hare cannot win no matter what, print −1-1−1.


Input Format

The first line of input will contain a single integer TTT, denoting the number of test cases.
The first and only line of each test case contains three space-separated integers L,V1,L, V_1,L,V1​, and V2V_2V2​ — the length of the racetrack, the tortoise's speed, and the hare's speed.


Output Format
For each test case, output on a new line the longest time the hare can wait, while still being able to win the race.

sampleTestCases.explanation:
Explanation:
Test case 111: Here, L=10,V1=2,V2=3L = 10, V_1 = 2, V_2 = 3L=10,V1​=2,V2​=3.
The tortoise takes 555 seconds to finish the race, while the hare takes 3133\frac{1}{3}331​ seconds; which rounds up to 444.
So, the hare cannot wait at all before starting.
Test case 222: The tortoise still takes 555 seconds, but the hare now takes only 2.52.52.5 seconds, which rounds up to 333.
The hare can now wait one second before starting and still finish strictly ahead.
Test case 333: The tortoise takes 555 seconds to finish the race, while the hare takes 333 seconds.
Again, the hare can wait for one second before starting.
Test case 555: The hare and the tortoise both take 333 seconds (after rounding) to finish.
The hare can't strictly win no matter what, so the answer is −1-1−1.
